basic_info.company: Valero Energy Corporation (operator), Matrix Service Industrial Contractors (contractor)

process_context.operating_conditions: Reactor undergoing nitrogen purge post-catalyst loading; slow continuous N2 flow through unsealed top manway; ambient temperature and atmospheric pressure

failure_mechanism.root_cause: Failure to recognize and control nitrogen-induced oxygen-deficient atmosphere outside the confined space opening due to inadequate hazard communication and isolation.
failure_mechanism.deviation_type: Oxygen deficiency in vicinity of equipment opening
failure_mechanism.causal_chain: Nitrogen purge initiated → N2 leaked from unsealed manway → displaced ambient air → O2 concentration dropped below 19.5% near opening → workers entered low-O2 zone without monitoring or PPE → asphyxiation → unplanned rescue → second fatality
failure_mechanism.physical_principles: Gas density-driven stratification and diffusion; inert gas displacement of breathable air; silent, odorless, non-irritating nature of nitrogen leading to undetected hypoxia

existing_safeguards: CSB-referenced Valero safety procedures requiring warning signs/barricades
CHSC temporary tarp and danger tape
protection_system.safeguard_failures: No nitrogen-specific warning sign or physical barricade installed per procedure; danger tape and tarp provided insufficient hazard identification for nitrogen asphyxiation risk; no continuous O2 monitoring at manway perimeter; rescue plan absent for nitrogen-purged equipment
csb_recommendations: Implement mandatory nitrogen hazard awareness training for all personnel working near inerted equipment
Require physical barricades and nitrogen-specific warning signage whenever inert gas purging is active—even outside confined space boundaries
Prohibit unauthorized entry near open purged equipment and enforce rescue-by-qualified-team-only policy

embedding_text: Nitrogen purge of hydrocracker reactor R1 created undetected oxygen-deficient atmosphere around its unsealed top manway. Workers approached the opening without monitoring, inhaled hypoxic air, and rapidly lost consciousness. Root cause was failure to recognize nitrogen's hazard beyond confined space boundaries. Two contractor fatalities occurred: first worker entering to retrieve tape, second attempting untrained rescue. No alarm, no signage, no O2 verification.
